impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense applies when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., in air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating the nonstaple goods defense. This decision reinforces antitrust liability for patent holders who leverage patent rights to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF benefit from clarity that patent holders cannot use licensing agreements to tie unpatented product purchases, preserving market access for rival suppliers of complementary goods.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust exposure when structuring exclusive supply arrangements tied to patent licenses, even where the patented and unpatented products are used in the same industry. Companies must carefully evaluate whether tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls to a model are abstract under step one of the Alice framework, and whether damages based on foreign sales are recoverable for domestic patent infringement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict for Columbia, holding that the asserted cybersecurity patent claims were directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. This decision narrows the scope of patentable subject matter for software-based security methods and limits extraterritorial damages recovery.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies facing patent assertions in cybersecurity and software detection benefit from the court's application of Alice to invalidate claims framed as detecting anomalies using models, and from the limitation on foreign-sales damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in cybersecurity and software detection face heightened risk that their claims will be deemed abstract. Universities and research institutions licensing such patents may see reduced damages if foreign sales are excluded.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents claiming server-based content tailoring for device capabilities were ineligible under § 101, reversing a jury verdict of infringement and $2.5 million damages award against Netflix. The court found the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content delivery to device specifications. This decision significantly impacts streaming technology patent enforcement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major streaming and technology companies gain protection against patent assertions covering common adaptive streaming functionality, reducing licensing costs and litigation exposure for widely deployed content delivery technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering adaptive content delivery and streaming media face increased invalidity risk under § 101, potentially devaluing significant patent assets in the streaming technology space.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent procurement and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (which had been granted against Tenaris) and Walker Process fraud (which had been granted in favor of Tenaris), finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. The case involves coiled tubing patents where Tenaris allegedly failed to disclose prior CYMAX technology documents during prosecution. The decision sends both the patent enforceability and antitrust claims to trial, with significant implications for the oil and gas industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence on factual development before summary resolution, and patent challengers gain the opportunity to pursue Walker Process antitrust claims at trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face prolonged litigation uncertainty when prior art disclosure issues are raised, and the potential for Walker Process antitrust liability creates substantial financial exposure beyond mere patent invalidity.

impacts.caseName: Genuine Enabling Technology LLC v. Sony Group Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's failure to account for all elements of a means-plus-function corresponding structure in its infringement analysis warrants summary judgment of noninfringement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of noninfringement in favor of Sony, holding that GET and its expert failed to adequately map all elements of the disclosed corresponding structure for a means-plus-function 'encoding means' limitation to Sony's PlayStation controllers and consoles. The decision reinforces the rigorous requirements for proving infringement of means-plus-function claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against means-plus-function patent claims benefit from the court's strict requirement that plaintiffs must account for every element of the corresponding structure, raising the bar for infringement theories.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders asserting means-plus-function claims face heightened risk of summary judgment if their infringement analysis does not meticulously map every element of the corresponding structure disclosed in the specification.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) in the original declaratory judgment action may obtain such a stay by dismissing and refiling the same action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) where BioMarin had missed the deadline to seek a stay in the original action. The court found that allowing dismissal and refiling to restart the statutory clock would circumvent Congress's time-limited stay provision. This decision is significant for pharmaceutical companies coordinating ITC proceedings and district court patent litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers (particularly pharmaceutical companies seeking declaratory judgments) are protected from indefinite delays caused by ITC respondents gaming the mandatory stay provision through strategic refiling of actions.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC complainants and patent holders lose the ability to reset the mandatory stay clock by having opponents refile declaratory judgment actions, requiring more careful litigation planning when pursuing parallel ITC and district court proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement functionality violated Section 337 by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's limited exclusion order barring importation of certain Apple Watch models, finding no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, patent validity rulings, or infringement findings regarding Masimo's wearable pulse oximetry patents. This high-profile decision sustains a significant import ban on one of the world's most popular consumer electronics products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in wearable health technology are strengthened in their ability to enforce patents through ITC exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies face substantial business disruption from ITC exclusion orders when incorporating patented health-monitoring technologies into flagship products, potentially forcing costly design-arounds or licensing agreements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function patent claims are indefinite when the specification describes the corresponding structure using terms like 'conventional in design and operation' without disclosing detailed internal circuitry or algorithms.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of Gramm's crop harvester height-control patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification's description of 'controller interface,' 'head controller,' and 'hydraulic control system' as 'conventional in design and operation' sufficiently disclosed corresponding structure for the means-plus-function 'control means' limitation. The case was remanded for further proceedings on infringement against Deere.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more flexible standard for disclosing corresponding structure for means-plus-function claims, particularly when the technology involves conventional components well understood in the art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies like Deere defending against means-plus-function claims face reduced ability to challenge patent validity on indefiniteness grounds when specifications reference conventional components without granular detail.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report on networking patents asserted against Microsoft's Azure platform and vacated the resulting summary judgment for Microsoft. The court found the district court erred in its Daubert analysis and its refusal to allow Exafer to reopen discovery. This decision provides important guidance on the admissibility of patent damages evidence and the consequences of discovery rulings on case disposition.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to reverse overly aggressive Daubert exclusions of damages evidence, preserving their ability to present damages theories to juries.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defendants like Microsoft who obtained summary judgment through Daubert exclusion of damages evidence face renewed exposure as cases are remanded for further proceedings on damages.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement of spinal surgery patents and in granting JMOL to the defendant.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Barry's technical and damages experts and the resulting JMOL for DePuy, finding the district court applied overly restrictive standards for expert testimony on induced infringement of surgical method patents. The case involves patents on en bloc derotation techniques for spinal deformity correction. The decision was remanded for a new trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Individual inventors and small patent holders asserting method patents against large medical device companies benefit from more permissive standards for expert testimony on induced infringement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed litigation exposure and potential damages when appellate courts reverse expert exclusion orders that had been dispositive of infringement claims.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's ruling that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute governing confidential treatment (19 U.S.C. §§ 1516a, 1677f) and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The decision requires the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than applying automatic designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries, trade associations, and the public gain greater access to trade investigation data, improving transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings and enabling more informed participation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign manufacturers and exporters who submit questionnaire responses to the ITC face reduced confidentiality protections, potentially exposing competitively sensitive business information. The ITC faces increased administrative burden in making individualized confidentiality assessments.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement and associated attorneys' fees and incentive awards in a nationwide class action challenging excessive PACER fees charged by the federal judiciary.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging the federal judiciary exceeded its statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations. The court rejected objections to subject matter jurisdiction, settlement fairness, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to the nonprofit class representatives. The settlement provides full or substantial reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users.
impacts.positiveImplications: PACER users, including businesses, law firms, and organizations that regularly access federal court records, benefit from substantial fee reimbursement and prospective restraints on excessive fee collection.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical-electrical connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's motion for JMOL of obviousness and new trial on damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial trees with simultaneous mechanical and electrical trunk connections. The decision upholds the jury's validity and damages findings, reinforcing patent protection for consumer product innovations in the seasonal decorative products market.
impacts.positiveImplications: Consumer product innovators benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on non-obviousness, protecting incremental but commercially significant innovations in competitive product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors face continued exclusion from markets for patented product features where jury verdicts sustain validity, even when prior art arguably taught individual elements of the claimed combination.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous observation of multimedia presentations are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed dismissal of a patent infringement complaint against Google, holding that the asserted web conferencing patent claims were ineligible under § 101. The claims covered systems enabling participants to observe web conferencing sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion at different playback rates. This decision further narrows patent eligibility for software-based collaboration and communication technologies.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies operating web conferencing and collaboration platforms benefit from reduced patent assertion risk for features involving time-shifting and asynchronous content viewing.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering web conferencing and multimedia streaming technologies face additional invalidity exposure under § 101, reducing the value of these patent assets.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding the Board's exercise of discretionary IPR non-institution authority are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions and that the instructions do not create binding norms. This ruling preserves the Director's broad discretion to control IPR institution through internal guidance.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to manage IPR institution decisions through flexible internal guidance without the constraints of formal rulemaking, preserving greater predictability in the patent system.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and frequent IPR petitioners like Apple face continued uncertainty about IPR institution discretion, with limited ability to challenge PTO policies that may result in more frequent non-institution decisions.
